note: Non-clinical, evidence-informed coping and CBT-style micro-actions for a burnout check-in coach. No diagnosis, no clinical labels, no crisis methods. Levels: green=tracking well, yellow=early strain, red=heavy load, any=fits any level.

snippets.text: Pick the single most important task for the next hour and let everything else wait. One clear thing beats five half-started ones.
If you can, close the laptop for the rest of today. Rest is part of the work, not a reward you have to earn first.
Write down the three things draining you most right now. Naming them shrinks them from a fog into a list you can actually act on.
You're holding steady. Protect one small window today that's just for you, before something else fills it.
Trade one meeting or task today for a 20-minute walk. Moving your body often resets your head faster than pushing through does.
Send one message to a person you trust and tell them you're running low. You don't need the right words, just an honest one.
Ask yourself what would still matter in a week. Spend your best energy there and let the rest be good enough.
Tonight, put your phone across the room before bed. A few feet of distance makes the scroll harder and sleep easier.
If your mind races at night, keep a notepad by the bed and write the thought down. Parking it on paper tells your brain it's safe to let go.
Try dimming the lights an hour before bed tonight. Cooler, softer light is a quiet signal to your body that the day is winding down.
Aim to be in bed 30 minutes earlier tonight, even if you don't sleep right away. Lying still in the dark still gives your system a break.
If you wake at 3am, resist checking the time or your phone. Slow your breathing out longer than you breathe in, and let the night carry you.
Keep your wake-up time steady, even after a rough night. A consistent morning anchor does more for energy than a long lie-in.
Set a timer for 25 minutes on one task and silence notifications. When it rings, you're allowed to stop. Starting is the hard part, not finishing.
Before you dive in, jot the one outcome you want from this work block. A clear target keeps your attention from drifting.
If you can't focus, do the smallest possible version of the task first. A two-minute start often unsticks the whole thing.
When your brain feels too full to think, stop and empty it onto paper for five minutes. You can't focus through fog you haven't cleared.
Close the tabs you're not using right now. A cluttered screen quietly pulls at attention you'd rather spend elsewhere.
If the same worry keeps interrupting you, schedule 10 minutes to think about it later today. Then gently return to the task in front of you.
Try a slow breath: in for four counts, out for six. A longer exhale tells your nervous system the alarm can quiet down.
Name five things you can see and three you can hear right now. Grounding in the room helps when your thoughts are racing ahead of you.
Ask whether the worry is something you can act on or only something you can fear. If you can act, take one small step; if not, set it down for now.
Feeling a little keyed up is normal. A few minutes of slow breathing or a short stretch can keep it from building.
If reaching out feels heavy, start with a small message to one safe person. Connection eases anxiety more reliably than waiting it out alone.
Notice the story your mind is telling, then ask what a kind friend would say back. Speaking to yourself gently is a skill, not a weakness.
Put both feet flat on the floor and feel the chair holding you. Sometimes the fastest way out of your head is back into your body.
Drink a full glass of water and step into daylight for a few minutes. Mild dehydration and dim rooms quietly sap energy you didn't know you were losing.
Lower the bar for today on purpose. Doing the essentials and resting is a complete day when your tank is this low.
Match the task to your energy: save the hard thinking for your sharpest hour and give the dull tasks to the slow ones.
A short burst of movement, even two minutes of stretching, can lift a flat afternoon more than another coffee will.
If you keep hitting an afternoon wall, a 15-minute rest with your eyes closed can be more restoring than pushing through it.
Eat something with a little protein if it's been a while. Steady fuel keeps your mood and focus from dipping with your blood sugar.
Break the thing that feels huge into the very next physical step. You don't have to see the whole staircase to take one stair.
Choose one thing to drop or delay today and let it go without guilt. Protecting your limits is how you keep showing up at all.
Try a brain dump: list everything on your mind, then circle just one to start. Getting it out of your head frees up room to think.
Things feel manageable right now. A quick five-minute plan for tomorrow will keep it that way and ease the morning rush.
If your plate is overflowing, ask one person for help with one thing. Sharing the load is a strength, not an admission.
When everything feels urgent, pause and ask what truly can't wait until tomorrow. Usually it's far less than it feels in the moment.
Reach out to one person today, even just a short hello. Connection often feels hardest right when it would help the most.
Tell someone you trust that you're pulling back and don't quite know why. You don't have to explain it perfectly to be heard.
Plan one low-effort bit of company this week, like a walk or a quiet coffee. Small contact counts when big plans feel like too much.
You're doing okay. Keep one regular point of contact on the calendar so connection doesn't slip away when you get busy.
If you've been assuming people are annoyed with you, check the evidence rather than the fear. Your mind tends to fill silence with the worst story.
When a thought loops, name it out loud: 'I'm replaying this again.' Catching the loop is the first step to stepping out of it.
Shift from 'why is this happening' to 'what's one small thing I can do next.' Action quiets a spinning mind better than analysis does.
Give the worry a 10-minute window later, then redirect now. Telling your mind 'not yet, but soon' loosens its grip without ignoring it.
If a heavy thought won't quiet down tonight, write it out and add one kind line you'd tell a friend in the same spot.
A little overthinking is human. A short walk or a change of room can break a mild loop before it settles in.
Take one slow breath before you decide your next move. A single pause can change a reaction into a choice.
Step away from the screen for five minutes and look at something far away. Short, regular breaks keep you steadier than long ones forced by a crash.
Be gentle with yourself today; you're carrying a lot. Pick one kind thing to do for yourself and let that be enough.
You're in a good spot to build a small habit. Choose one tiny daily anchor, like a morning glass of water, and let it carry you on harder days.
